title: SCALPEL (Sars-Cov-2 Antibody response in oLder PEopLe) [COVID-19]
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"e8ogh","text":"Severe Acute Respriatory Syndrome coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2) remains a considerable risk to high risk populations, including older adults. The humoral immune response dynamics to infection and/or immunisation in older adults remains unclear. This protocol decribes the serological methods employed in the Sars-Cov-2 Antibody response in oLder PEopLe (SCALPEL) study which aims to evaulate the humoral immune response, taking into account symptomatic and asymptomatic infection through regular weekly PCR testing supported by SARS-CoV-2 anti-nucleocapsid antibody testing, as well as response to vaccination by testing with both a commerical SARS-CoV-2 total IgG and inhouse anti-receptor binding domain double antigen binding immunoassay. Further more, antibody acitivity tested by an inhouse pseudovirus neutralisation assay, and avidity utilising an inhouse competitive ELISA, are also conducted in order to evaluate potential correlates of protection.  ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"brxxr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"nsqcg","text":"Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2) spread extensively following its recognition in late 2019, becoming a global pandemic by March 2020 and with over 100,000,000 reported cases and over 2,000,000 attributed deaths worldwide.1 Across many countries, significant public health isolation measures have been adopted in an attempt to slow the spread of infection. Older people, in particular those aged 70 years or more, constitute a high-risk population for COVID-19 associated morbidity and mortality. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":249,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cbpuh","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"s8f4g","text":"Four major structural proteins have been identified (spike, envelope, nucleocapsid, and membrane). Initial B cell response in SARS-CoV-2 infection appears to occur prior to the recovery phase,5 and while antibody responses are thought to arise first to the nucleocapsid in SARS-CoV-1, it is the spike protein (essential in virus attachment and cell entry) and specifically the receptor-binding-domain (RBD) for which neutralizing antibodies have been identified.6 Early work has also suggested anti-RBD antibodies could have some correlation with neutralization in SARS-CoV-2.7 In interpretation of SARS-CoV-2 serology, information around potentially inferred immunity has been vague and at times exaggerated in the context of current scientific understanding. As it stands, the degree of immunity conferred from infection with SARS-CoV-2 remains unknown. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":192,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":462,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":576,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"v8tqx","text":" ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"wensh","text":"The UK national COVID-19 vaccination program began in December 2020. The population was stratified into groups based on COVID-19 associated mortality and morbidity. Early vaccination was performed with the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 Vaccine which requires two doses 3 weeks apart. This vaccination schedule was adapted by the UK government following advice from the Chief Medical Officer and the second dose was delayed to 12 weeks after receiving the first dose. There has been some criticism of this as data to support prolongation of the intra-vaccine period is lacking. In September 2021 the Joint Committee on Vaccination and Immunisation (JCVI) advised the UK government: “that for the 2021 COVID-19 booster vaccine programme individuals who received vaccination in Phase 1 of the COVID-19 vaccination programme (priority groups 1 to 9) should be offered a third dose COVID-19 booster vaccine. This includes: those living in residential care homes for older adults’. Recent vaccine trials recruited very few participants over the age of 70 despite this cohort representing the majority of deaths from COVID-19. Follow-up studies are now needed to determine the persistence of antibodies following first and second dose of the vaccine as well as determine the degree to which any sterilizing effect is provided, and determine the need for booster vaccinations in this population. In addition, a failure to identify seroconversion in a proportion of PCR positive cases should demand further caution in serology interpretation.9 ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":676,"length":299},{"style":"sup","offset":1528,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"krjm4","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"vvnzn","text":"This study will aim to characterise the RBD/NP antibody response in an older adult population following vaccination with the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 Vaccine and in doing so conduct exploration of concordance between various antibody-target assays. Through assessment of assay concordance and performance, preliminary findings are suggestive that a small proportion of participants will be identified that do not exhibit a detectable antibody response to SARS-CoV-2 infection. Antibody quality, through assessment of binding avidity, and its association with prior infection status and risk of subsequent infection will also be explored.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"yjiy9","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"oinui","text":"Exploration of immunological factors associated with fallure to mount a robust immune reponse to vaccination will be carried out with additional sampling in order to assist the development of social-distancing and risk policy in the event of potential future exposure to SARS-CoV-2 positive cases in such cases.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"fxif6","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
materials_text: {"blocks":[{"key":"5dxxm","text":"SARS-CoV-2 IgG II Quant Reagent Kit 6S60","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"gbul9","text":"Abbott SARS-CoV-2 IgG assay kit","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bh9hb","text":"ARCHITECT i System","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"omwwv","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Potential participants at the identified long-term care facility (LTCF) will be informed about the study and provided with the participant information leaflet and consent form. Suitable time will be afforded to consider this information before consent is sought. Clear direction will be provided that their decision to not participate will in no way negatively affect their care, the offer of a vaccine, or involvement in any future studies should they wish to partake.

Sample: 2x5ml vial of whole blood will be drawn. One vial will be sent within 1 working day to the centralised laboratory for further testing as per the study method. The second sample will be spun down and frozen, marked with the participant’s anonymous identification number and subject ID number and stored on site at -80 o C in keeping with all local regulations and approvals required for the long-term storage of blood samples in case of requirements to repeat the sample testing procedures.

All eligible participants will undergo venepuncture for serological ELISA testing, pseudovirus neutralisation and anti-receptor binding domain antibody avidity testing prior to SARS-CoV-2 vaccination. These results will be fed-back to the medical team at the LCTF for onward distribution to participants.

Individuals who have only received a single dose of the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 Vaccine will go on for repeat testing at one month. All participants who have received two doses of the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 Vaccine at the point of initial testing will exit the study after phase 1. Any individual who is unable to receive, or chooses not to receive, the second dose of the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 Vaccine will exit the study after Phase 1.

All participants recruited in phase 1 and since initial testing having received their second dose of the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 Vaccine will undergo a second round of venepuncture repeat testing as listed above 4 weeks after the second dose.

Participants were invited to undertake blood draw for assessment of SARS-CoV-2 antibodies at three intervals, namely (i) as part of an initial outbreak investigation and coinciding with the end of the UK’s first wave of infection and relaxation of initial social distancing regulations (June 2020) and (ii) prior to receipt of the Pfizer BioNTech BNT162b2 vaccine (December 2020) and (iii) four weeks after the second dose of Pfizer BioNTech BNT162b2 vaccine (April 2021). Antibody wane over eight months in older adults (using the same assay, see below) 10 informed the decision to test at least once, but not more than, every 6 months for evidence of new seroconversion, while not unnecessarily increasing sampling burden to participants. Likewise, in order to reduce test burden on participants, maximise study retention and in line with UK vaccine strategy for an initial two-vaccine regimen, a separate blood draw after a single vaccine dose was not carried out.

Antibodies to the SARS-CoV-2 nucleocapsid protein (anti-NP) may be detected in serum following infection but not in response to immunisation. Assessment for evidence of seroconversion following SARS-CoV-2 infection (symptomatic or asymptomatic) was via an anti-nucleocapsid (anti-NP) IgG chemiluminescent microparticle immunoassay (CMIA) as per manufacture instructions (Abbot Laboratories, Lake Bluff, IL, USA). Anti-receptor binding domain (anti-RBD) antibodies have been previously shown to be predictive of neutralising response. 11 In order to further characterise the response to prior SARS-CoV-2 infection and vaccination, paired serum samples pre- and post-vaccine were measured using a quantitative hybrid in-house anti-RBD double antigen binding assay (hybrid-DABA) (Imperial College, London, UK) as previously described. 11 The hybrid-DABA utilises S1 antigen manipulated to express stably RBD epitopes and enable highly specific capture of anti-RBD antibody, detected by labelled fluid phase horseradish peroxidase-coupled RBD. Hybrid-DABA binding ratios are converted and reported in binding antibody units/ml (BAU/ml), allowing correlation with World Health Organisation (WHO) international standards for SARS-CoV-2 IgG binding antibody units. Samples were also tested on the commercial quantitative Abbott Architect IgG Quant II CMIA (Abbot Laboratories, Lake Bluff, IL, USA) in order to observe comparison with a commercially available assay marketed to assess SARS-CoV-2 antibodies, including neutralizing antibodies. The Abbott CMIA is a two-step automated immunoassay for qualitative and quantitative assessment of total SARS-CoV-2 IgG antibodies, including those to the S1 subunit. Abbott arbitrary units (AU/ml) are multiplied by a factor of 0.142, giving a binding antibody unit (BAU)/ml result, providing correlation with the WHO international standards. 12

Post-vaccine in vitro antibody neutralisation activity was further explored by pseudotype virus assay, with comparison of results between SARS-CoV-2 infection naïve individuals and those with prior evidence of SARS-CoV-2 infection (both asymptomatic and symptomatic). A lentivirus that encodes for luciferase and pseudo-typed with the spike of SARS-CoV-2 was utilised. In brief, 16µl participant sera was heat inactivated (56 o C, 30 mins) and initial dilutions made with 5µl HI serum and 2% growth medium (DMEM, penicillin/streptomycin, 2% FBS). Following this, a total of eight serial dilutions were made and mixed with PsV (0.5-1 x 10^6 RLU/well) and incubated at 37 o C for 48 hrs. Luciferase was then measured at 48 hrs post-infection (BrightGlo, Promega). An initial 1:40 dilution was carried out and data expressed as a percentage neutralisation e.g. 100% meaning serum neutralised 100% of the PsV, as previously described. 13

An inhouse competitive anti-RBD antibody competitive enzyme linked immunosorbent assay was utilised to assess antibody binding avidity maturation. As part of the consent process, participants were asked for permission to utilise any remaining serum taken for serological and pseudovirus neutralisation testing on additional SARS-CoV-2 antibody assays as they became available. Those individuals that had sufficient paired sample remaining from pre- and post-vaccine sampling and had given permission were assessed for antibody binding avidity at these time points. Assay methodology has previously been made available. 14 Solid phase 96-microwells plates (NUNC Immunomodule, U8 Maxisorp wells) were coated with 100 μl of S1 antigen at a concentration of 5 μg/mL (MicroImmune Coating Buffer; ClinTech, Guildford, UK) overnight at 2−8 °C, followed by 3 h at 35−37 °C (under moist conditions) and 1 h at room temperature. Plates were washed once with 0.05 % Tween 20/PBS, blocked with MicroImmune Blocking Solution (3−4 hours at 37 °C in a moist box), aspirated, dried overnight at 37 °C and stored dry at 4 °C in sealed pouches with desiccant. The assay was carried out by simultaneous addition of 25μl of sera and 75ul of recombinant anti-RBD neutralizing monoclonal antibody (MAB12444; Native Antigen, Oxford, UK) conjugated with HRP, diluted in conjugant buffer (Conjugant Diluent; ClinTech, Guildford, UK) supplemented with 10% FBS and 10% Normal Human Plasma to each well. Plates were incubated for 1 h at 37 °C then washed five times with wash buffer (ClinTech, Guildford, UK). One hundred microlitres of TMB substrate was then added (ClinTech, Guildford, UK), incubated for 30 min at 37 °C, after which the reaction was stopped and read spectrometrically at 450−630 nm. Results were calculated as a percentage of the average of optical density (OD) obtained for three negative controls assayed in each run.
On any test failures will initially result in re-running of the test from the original sample. Checks will be made, including of internal and external controls as per the manufacturer's guidelines.

1. Detection in anti-nucleocapsid antibody and anti-receptor binding domain antibody following infection and/or immunisation, with rising titre on boosting/further exposure to SARS-CoV-2 antigen. 2. Increasing anti-receptor binding domain neutralisation and antibody avidity with subsequent antigen exposure.
